questions.question: Why is adding a DLQ only the beginning of the design?
options: Because operators still need classification, inspection, replay, and discard controls.
Because DLQs automatically fix malformed events.
Because a DLQ guarantees no duplicate side effects.
Because every message should always be replayed immediately.
questions.explanation: DLQ storage without control-plane workflows is incomplete.

questions.question: What question most changes replay safety?
options: Whether replay can retrigger side effects such as sends, writes, or billing actions.
Whether the DLQ dashboard has light mode.
Whether producers use protobuf or JSON comments.
Whether operators prefer alphabetical topic lists.
questions.explanation: Side-effect risk is central to replay design.

questions.question: Which signal best shows that replay is harming the live system?
options: Live-consumer lag rising while replay throughput increases.
Average replay-request title length.
The number of runbook pages in the wiki.
Median TLS handshake time for operator laptops.
questions.explanation: Replay must be evaluated against its impact on live traffic.

questions.question: Why is a dry-run estimate valuable before replay?
options: It previews scope and expected impact before operators inject load or duplicate side effects.
It guarantees the replay will succeed without errors.
It removes the need for actor attribution.
It replaces all need for DLQ retention.
questions.explanation: Dry run lowers blast radius and improves operator judgment.

questions.question: What trade-off comes with longer DLQ retention?
options: Better recovery and investigation at the cost of storage and policy burden.
Guaranteed lower live-traffic latency.
Elimination of poison messages.
No need for cleanup workflows.
questions.explanation: Retention is useful but not free.

questions.question: Which failure is most directly mitigated by requiring scoped replay filters and actor identity?
options: An operator accidentally replaying too much data without accountability.
A worker host losing NTP sync.
A dashboard using too many colors.
A queue partition becoming hot from normal traffic.
questions.explanation: Scoped, auditable replay reduces operator blast radius.

questions.question: Why should replay usually use isolated workers or quotas?
options: Because recovery traffic can otherwise starve live delivery paths.
Because replayed messages cannot be stored in the main system.
Because isolation removes the need for debugging metadata.
Because quotas make DLQs unnecessary.
questions.explanation: Replay is often bursty and dangerous to shared capacity.

questions.question: What design change most improves the usefulness of a DLQ during incident response?
options: Attach structured failure reason and last-processing context to each dead-lettered message.
Store messages without metadata to reduce write cost.
Delete old failures as soon as a new failure arrives.
Hide replay history from operators to simplify the UI.
questions.explanation: Triage depends on rich failure context.
